title: James Hoffmann V60: The Ultimate Pour-Over ☕📈
description:
James Hoffmann's V60 technique broke pour-over into a global phenomenon. The exact recipe: ratio, grind, water temp, pour schedule. Why it works and how to nail it at home.

Sources: James Hoffmann YouTube channel, Square Mile Coffee Roasters, SCA brewing standards, Hario.

segments.voice: One YouTube video changed home coffee brewing forever. James Hoffmann's V60 technique tutorial has over four million views. The method became the global default for specialty pour-over. The recipe is simple but the details matter. Get one wrong and the cup is muddy. Get them right and the coffee tastes like a different beverage.

The V60 dripper was designed by Hario, a Japanese glass company, in 2004. The name comes from the sixty-degree cone angle and the V shape. The single large hole at the bottom lets the brewer control flow rate. The spiral ridges allow air to escape during brewing.

Before pre-rinsing your filter, set up the equipment. You need a V60 dripper, a paper filter, a server or mug, a gooseneck kettle for pour control, a scale that displays seconds, fresh coffee, good water, and a grinder. The total setup costs about one hundred fifty dollars at entry level.

Hoffmann recommends a coffee to water ratio of one to sixty by weight for V60. Fifteen grams of coffee with two hundred fifty grams of water. The ratio produces a balanced cup. Stronger drinkers can go one to fifteen for a denser cup. Lighter drinkers can stretch to one to eighteen.

Grind size matters as much as ratio. Hoffmann uses medium-fine for V60, slightly finer than table salt. The exact setting depends on your grinder. The grind should produce a brew time around three minutes total. Too fast means too coarse. Too slow means too fine. Adjust by one setting at a time.
segments.visual: coffee grind size V60 medium fine

segments.voice: Water temperature should sit between ninety-two and ninety-four Celsius for most coffees. Boiling water at one hundred Celsius extracts too aggressively, producing bitterness. Water below ninety Celsius under-extracts, producing sourness. Variable temperature kettles solve this problem easily.

Pre-rinse the paper filter with hot water before adding coffee. This rinse removes paper taste from the filter and warms the brewer. Discard the rinse water from the server. Place the dripper back on the server and add ground coffee. Tap the dripper to level the grounds.

The bloom phase starts the brew. Pour two times the coffee weight in water, so thirty grams of water for fifteen grams of coffee. Pour in slow circles to wet all grounds evenly. The coffee releases CO2 trapped from roasting. The grounds will swell and bubble. Wait forty-five seconds.

After the bloom, the main pour begins. Hoffmann uses a two-stage continuous pour. Pour up to sixty percent of total water by the one minute mark. Slow steady spirals from center outward. Then pour the remaining water to reach total target by one minute thirty seconds.

After the second pour, give the slurry a gentle swirl. This evens out the coffee bed and dislodges any grounds stuck to the sides. The brew then drains through the filter. Total brew time from start to drip cessation should hit three minutes to three minutes fifteen seconds.

If the brew finishes faster than three minutes, the grind is too coarse. Adjust finer by one setting. If the brew takes longer than three minutes thirty, the grind is too fine. Adjust coarser by one setting. One adjustment per brew until you hit the target time.

The pour technique matters. Use small, slow spirals starting from the center and moving outward, then back inward. Avoid pouring directly on the paper filter, which bypasses the coffee bed. Keep the water stream thin and controlled. A gooseneck kettle is essential for this control.

Water quality dramatically affects the cup. Hoffmann recommends water with one hundred fifty parts per million total dissolved solids, with calcium and magnesium in balance. Chlorinated tap water destroys delicate flavors. Bottled water like Volvic or filtered tap with calcium adjustments work well.

Different coffees need different parameters. Light roast Ethiopian lots benefit from slightly higher water temperature, ninety-four Celsius. Dark roast Brazilian beans prefer ninety Celsius to avoid bitterness. Hoffmann adjusts grind size and temperature based on origin. Start with the standard, then refine.

The number of pours debate divides V60 enthusiasts. Hoffmann uses two pours total: bloom plus continuous pour. Tetsu Kasuya uses five pours in his 4:6 method. Some Japanese baristas use ten or more pulse pours. Each technique extracts differently. Start with two pours and experiment later.

The Hoffmann V60 technique extracts somewhere around twenty to twenty-two percent extraction yield, measured by a refractometer. The total dissolved solids in the cup sit around one point three to one point four percent. These numbers represent the SCA gold cup standard for filter coffee.

Filter paper choice changes the cup. The standard Hario tabbed filters are bleached white. The unbleached brown Hario filters produce slightly different flavor due to paper composition. Cafec abaca filters use abaca fiber for faster drainage. Hoffmann tested all of them. Standard white Hario works best.

The plastic V60 versus ceramic V60 versus glass V60 debate matters less than people think. Hoffmann tested temperature retention across materials. The differences are small. The ceramic V60 retains heat best if pre-warmed. The plastic V60 is most durable and travel-friendly. Pick whichever you prefer.

The single cup V60 size, called 01, brews up to fifteen grams of coffee. The two cup V60, called 02, brews up to thirty grams of coffee. The larger 03 size brews up to forty-five grams. The drainage characteristics differ across sizes, slightly affecting flavor.

Common V60 mistakes include using stale coffee, pouring too fast, pouring on the paper filter wall, not pre-rinsing the filter, using boiling water, grinding too coarse, and not using a scale. Each mistake produces a measurable change in cup quality. Eliminating mistakes matters more than perfecting technique.

The Hoffmann technique requires fresh coffee. Beans should be roasted within four weeks for best results. Coffee older than four weeks has lost most CO2, reducing bloom and changing extraction. Buy from a local roaster that prints roast dates, not best-by dates.

Grinder quality affects V60 results more than the dripper itself. A burr grinder produces uniform particle size, essential for even extraction. Blade grinders produce inconsistent grounds that brew unevenly. Entry-level options like the Baratza Encore start around one hundred fifty dollars.

The V60 method excels at clean cups with bright aromatics. The paper filter removes oils and fines that would muddy the cup. This makes V60 the preferred brewer for light roast specialty coffees from Ethiopia, Kenya, and Colombia. Dark roast coffees often brew better in French press or moka pot.
segments.visual: V60 clean cup aromatic light roast

segments.voice: Brewing at altitude requires adjustments. Water boils at lower temperatures at high altitudes. Denver at five thousand feet boils water at about ninety-five Celsius. La Paz Bolivia boils water at about eighty-eight Celsius. Adjust grind finer and brew time longer to compensate at altitude.

But there is something pour-over enthusiasts rarely mention. The V60 method produces a very particular style of cup: bright, clean, aromatic, light-bodied. Some coffees taste better in immersion methods like French press or AeroPress. The V60 is not universally the best brewer. It is the best brewer for clean, light extractions.

To master Hoffmann V60, weigh everything, time everything, taste critically, and adjust one variable at a time. Watch his original video. Buy fresh quality coffee. The cup will dramatically exceed any drip machine. Next video, the Tetsu Kasuya 4:6 method that wins championships. Subscribe.
